coredata.dc:title: Enhanced inhibition of calcium phosphate scale by polyaspartic acid modified with sulfur-nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Yijie
Zhang, Daquan
Liu, Dongdong
Hou, Jiawei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel hybrid scale inhibitor, sulfur‑nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots modified polyaspartic acid (SN-CDs/PASP), was developed to mitigate calcium phosphate (CaP), an area that remains largely underexplored. The synthesis and properties of the SN-CDs/PASP composite were characterized using Raman, FTIR, XPS, and TEM. Static scale inhibition experiments and electrochemical tests revealed that the composite significantly outperforms pure PASP, achieving a maximum inhibition rate of 98 % at 10 mg/L with an optimized Polysuccinimide (PSI):SN-CDs ratio of 10:1. Unlike traditional scale inhibitors such as phosphonates or ordinary PASP, which lack multifunctionality and exhibit limited specific surface area, the SN-CDs/PASP composite offers increased surface area due to the nanoscale SN-CDs, enhancing ion chelation. Compared to other nanomaterial-based inhibitors, such as common carbon quantum dots, the sulfonic and amino groups from SN-CDs further improve chelation and electrostatic repulsion, disrupting CaP crystal formation. Additionally, its fluorescence intensity correlates linearly with concentration, enabling real-time dosage monitoring—a feature absent in conventional inhibitors. This environmentally friendly composite provides new insights into advanced, high-efficiency scale inhibition for industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Modified polyaspartic acid
Calcium phosphate
Carbon quantum dot
Desalination
Sulfur-nitrogen co-doped

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Raman spectrum of SN-CDs (a); FTIR spectrum of SN-CDs and SN-CDs/PASP (b).
XPS spectra of SN-CDs. The full scan survey spectrum (a). High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (b), O 1s (c), N 1s (d) and S 2p (e).
XPS spectra of SN-CDs/PASP. The full scan survey spectrum (a). High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (b), O 1s (c), N 1s (d) and S 2p (e).
Representative TEM images of SN-CDs (a) and SN-CDs/PASP (c); size (diameter) distribution of SN-CDs (b) and SN-CDs/PASP (d).
Zeta potential of PASP and SN-CDs/PASP at different pH points (a); specific surface area comparison of PASP and SN-CDs/PASP (b).

The photoluminescence spectra of SN-CDs (a) and SN-CDs/PASP (b). The emission spectra of SN-CDs/PASP at 320 nm (c). Linear fitting result of emission intensity and concentration of SN-CDs/PASP (d). The UV–vis spectra of SN-CDs (e) and SN-CDs/PASP (f). The emission spectra of SN-CDs (g) and SN-CDs/PASP (h) after exposure to 365 nm UV light for varying durations.

Inhibition efficiency of PASP, SN-CDs, and SN-CDs/PASP at different ratios for Ca-P scale vs. inhibitor concentration (a). Inhibition efficiency of SN-CDs/PASP at different ratios for CaCO₃ and CaSO₄ (b). Nyquist plots (c) and Bode plots (d) of Al alloy in solution with different components of EIS measurements data.
XRD test patterns of Ca-P scales (a), and SEM images of Ca-P scales without scale inhibitor (b, b′); with PASP (c, c′); with SN-CDs/PASP (d, d′).
Mechanism of SN-CDs/PASP inhibition for Ca-P scale.
The fitted electrochemical impedance parameters for Al alloy in different solution without and with scale inhibitors.

head.ce:title: Enhanced inhibition of calcium phosphate scale by polyaspartic acid modified with sulfur-nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 201306, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Energy-Saving in Heat Exchange Systems, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 201306, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 201306, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel hybrid scale inhibitor, sulfur‑nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots modified polyaspartic acid (SN-CDs/PASP), was developed to mitigate calcium phosphate (CaP), an area that remains largely underexplored. The synthesis and properties of the SN-CDs/PASP composite were characterized using Raman, FTIR, XPS, and TEM. Static scale inhibition experiments and electrochemical tests revealed that the composite significantly outperforms pure PASP, achieving a maximum inhibition rate of 98 % at 10 mg/L with an optimized Polysuccinimide (PSI):SN-CDs ratio of 10:1. Unlike traditional scale inhibitors such as phosphonates or ordinary PASP, which lack multifunctionality and exhibit limited specific surface area, the SN-CDs/PASP composite offers increased surface area due to the nanoscale SN-CDs, enhancing ion chelation. Compared to other nanomaterial-based inhibitors, such as common carbon quantum dots, the sulfonic and amino groups from SN-CDs further improve chelation and electrostatic repulsion, disrupting CaP crystal formation. Additionally, its fluorescence intensity correlates linearly with concentration, enabling real-time dosage monitoring—a feature absent in conventional inhibitors. This environmentally friendly composite provides new insights into advanced, high-efficiency scale inhibition for industrial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Seawater desalination technology has emerged as a pivotal solution for ensuring a reliable supply of freshwater. Among the various desalination methods, low-temperature multi-effect distillation (LT-MED) is widely used due to its relatively low energy consumption compared to traditional multi-stage flash (MSF) distillation [1,2]. Nevertheless, scaling in heat exchanger tubes remains a significant challenge in the LT-MED process. This scaling, primarily caused by insoluble salts in seawater, is dominated by calcium salts, which form mineral deposits on heat exchanger surfaces [3,4].

In the LT-MED process, calcium phosphate (CaP) scale, mainly hydroxyapatite, contributes significantly to scaling. This scale is different from the more common calcium carbonate (CaC) scale. CaP exhibits a thermal conductivity of 1.5 W·m−1·K−1 between 25 °C and 900 °C, which is significantly lower than the 4.6 W·m−1·K−1 of CaC [5]. Given the low thermal conductivity of CaP, its deposition significantly reduces heat transfer efficiency. Since LT-MED requires a stable heat source, controlling CaP scaling is crucial for ensuring the process's efficiency. In recent years, nitrogen and phosphorus pollution in coastal waters have risen sharply, leading to eutrophication. This issue significantly raises the likelihood of CaP scale formation. As a result, managing CaP deposition in seawater desalination processes has become a major challenge. Compared to CaC, CaP is more difficult to eliminate using conventional acid cleaning methods. These deposits not only obstruct heat transfer but also cause corrosion, which negatively affects both the safety and economic viability of the system.

It is therefore imperative that the issue of scaling be addressed with urgency. Among the different methods for preventing scaling, scale inhibitors have attracted considerable interest because of their cost-effectiveness and convenience. Polyaspartic acid (PASP) represents the latest generation of green polymeric scale inhibitors. PASP has emerged as a promising, green, and non-toxic water treatment agent [6]. Due to its excellent scale inhibition properties and environmentally friendly degradation products, PASP has been widely studied in recent years. Many studies focus on the excellent chelating ability of PASP with metal ions, which has applications in various fields, such as wine preservation, soil stabilization, and carbon steel corrosion prevention [7]. Some improvements in scale inhibition have also been explored. For instance, Yu et al. [8] introduced 2-amino-benzimidazole (2-A) into PASP to develop a dual-function scale inhibitor, PASP-2-A, which exhibits good performance in both scale inhibition and corrosion inhibition. Chen et al. [9] synthesized a PASP/graphene oxide (GO) composite, which effectively enhanced dispersion and scale inhibition. However, PASP is not effective at preventing CaP scale formation, and limited research has been conducted on modifying PASP for this purpose. Therefore, developing an efficient PASP-based inhibitor for CaP scale is crucial.

In recent years, the novel carbon nanomaterials have emerged as promising candidates in water treatment applications [10–12]. These materials have high chemical reactivity, large surface areas, and good cost-effectiveness [13,14]. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) represent a distinctive category of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials among these [15–17]. With extremely high surface areas and abundant active edge sites, these particles have advantages over bulk and micro-sized materials in water treatment [18,19]. Their excellent fluorescence properties, good solubility in water, and low toxicity make them highly attractive [20]. Moreover, their ease of surface modification opens up opportunities for targeted solutions to specific problems [21,22]. Khan et al. [23] surface-functionalized CDs with PASP to create a biosensor (PAA-CD), which can selectively detect calcium (II) ions and glutathione (GSH). Surface-functionalized CDs, with their rich functional groups, enhance scale inhibition. Qu et al. [24] modified CDs with metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) to create the composite Tea-L-CDs@Ce-UiO-66-F4, demonstrating remarkable dye adsorption capacity within a short period. Many studies also use the fluorescence properties of CDs, which can be enhanced or quenched, to detect antibiotics and heavy metals in environmental and food samples [25–27]. Li et al. [28] reported an innovative study on sulfur quantum dots (CMC-SQDs) as a calcium sulfate scale inhibitor, modified with carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) to achieve excellent scale inhibition and antibacterial performance. This study underscores the potential of nanomaterial modification to enhance the functionality of scale inhibition and antibacterial properties, by guiding functional improvements in performance. Doping other elements into CDs for surface functionalization is a common strategy to enhance their performance [29,30]. Despite relevant experimental reports, the scale inhibition performance of single carbon dots still falls short compared to traditional inhibitors. Therefore, combining doped CDs with PASP may offer a new approach for inhibiting CaP scale.

In the course of this research, we introduced SN-CDs during the ring-opening polymerization of polysuccinimide (PSI) to synthesize polyaspartic acid (PASP), resulting in the successful formation of the SN-CDs/PASP composite scale inhibitor. The incorporation of sulfonic and amino groups in the SN-CDs enhances the inhibitor's ability to address the CaP scale problem, providing an innovative approach for improving scale control. The synthesized product was characterized using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). Static scale inhibition experiments and electrochemical impedance tests demonstrated its excellent effectiveness in inhibiting CaP scale. The inhibition mechanism was further investigated through X-ray Diffraction (XRD), surface area analysis, and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM).

The materials used in this study include polysuccinimide (Mw = 8000–10,000), calcium carboxylic acid indicator, sulphanilic acid, sulfuric acid, and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid which were supplied by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Additionally, sodium bicarbonate, citric acid, anhydrous sodium sulfate, potassium chloride, sodium tetraborate decahydrate, ammonium molybdate, antimony potassium tartrate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, L(+)-ascorbic acid, and calcium chloride were provided by Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

The synthesis of SN-CDs was modified based on previous studies [31–33]. Citric acid was used as the carbon source, while sulphanilic acid served as the sulfur and nitrogen source. In this procedure, 1.9 g of citric acid and 1.7 g of sulphanilic acid were mixed with 60 mL of deionized water and sonicated until fully dissolved. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the product was precipitated with ethanol and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min. The final product was dialyzed for 48 h, followed by drying at 70 °C for 24 h to obtain the SN-CDs.

Following the procedure outlined in previous studies [33], 0.6 g of Polysuccinimide (PSI) was added to 10 mL of deionized water. Then, 0.6 g of SN-CDs (at a 1:1 ratio) were added to the solution, and the corresponding amounts of SN-CDs were added for other ratios (2:1, 10:1). Subsequently, 10 mL of 1 M NaOH was added into the solution. The mixture was stirred and reacted at 60 °C for 24 h. Thereafter, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 6–7 using HCl, and the solution underwent precipitation with anhydrous ethanol for 12 h. Finally, the product was dried at 60 °C to obtain SN-CDs/PASP. The synthesis of the control group of PASP followed the same method, with the exception of the addition of SN-CDs. The synthesis reaction process is schematically represented in Fig. 1 .

The CaP scale inhibition test was conducted according to GB/T 6913–2023. The solution used in the experiment contained 250 mg/L of Ca2+ and 5 mg/L of PO₄3−. The reaction was maintained at 70 °C for 10 h to replicate the supersaturation conditions of CaP typically encountered in low-temperature multi-effect distillation desalination. After filtration, the concentration of PO₄3− in the filtrate was measured using the ammonium molybdate spectrophotometric method. The CaP scale inhibition efficiency (η, %) was then determined as follows:
(1)
η % =
ρ 1
−
ρ 0
ρ 2
−
ρ 0
× 100
where ρ₁ and ρ₀ (mg/mL) represent the residual PO₄3− concentrations in the supernatant after the 10-h test period with and without the inhibitor, respectively. ρ₂ (mg/mL) denotes the initial concentration of PO₄3−.

To test the broad applicability and multifunctional nature of SN-CDs/PASP, static scale inhibition experiments were conducted not only on CaP but also on calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) and calcium sulfate (CaSO₄), following the National Standard of China (GB/T 16632–2019). For CaSO₄, the solution contained 2000 mg/L of Ca2+ and 4800 mg/L of SO₄2−, and for CaCO₃, the solution contained 600 mg/L of Ca2+ and 2000 mg/L of HCO₃−.Both reactions were carried out at 70 °C for 10 h, followed by filtration to remove the formed scale. The supernatant was titrated with 0.01 mol/L EDTA to determine the Ca2+ concentration. The formula described above was also used to calculate the scale inhibition efficiency, where PO₄3− is replaced by Ca2+.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted on a CS310M electrochemical workstation. The electrodes, which were fabricated from a AA5052 aluminum alloy, exhibited an exposed surface area of 1 cm2. To prepare the electrodes, the experimental procedure involved the use of silicon carbide papers with varying grits, from 800 to 2000, for the abrasive polishing of the electrode surface, followed by cleaning with ethanol and drying at ambient temperature. Subsequent to this, impedance spectra were recorded within a frequency range spanning from 105 Hz to 10−2 Hz. The testing system consisted of 250 mg/L Ca2+ and 5 mg/L PO₄3− solutions, with a scale inhibitor concentration of 10 mg/L, at a reaction temperature of 70 °C, and a total volume of 100 mL. The electrochemical measurements were conducted after an open-circuit reaction time of 30 min, during which the system was allowed to stabilize. The impedance spectra were then recorded over a frequency range of 105 Hz to 10−2 Hz. EIS data were used to assess the inhibition efficiency by analyzing parameters such as resistance (R) and impedance modulus, which reflect the interaction between the scale-forming ions and the electrode surface.

The morphology of SN-CDs/PASP was observed using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and UV–vis spectra were measured with a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer and a U-2600 spectrophotometer, respectively. The surface elemental composition of SN-CDs and SN-CDs/PASP was analyzed using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). Raman spectroscopy was used to characterize the SN-CDs. Functional groups in SN-CDs/PASP were identified using an IS50 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR). The CaP structure was analyzed with a Bruker-D8 X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD). The specific surface area of PASP and SN-CDs/PASP was determined using BET analysis. Zeta Potential of PASP and SN-CDs/PASP was analyzed using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The surface morphology of CaP was examined using a Sigma 300 scanning electron microscope (SEM).

Fig. 2a presents the Raman spectrum of SN-CDs, which reveals two distinct peaks. The peak observed at 1372 cm−1 (D-band) is primarily attributed to the vibrations of carbon atoms located at lattice defects or edge positions. In contrast, the peak at 1567 cm−1 (G-band) indicates the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms in the graphitic structure. The ratio of peak intensities (ID/IG) is commonly employed to assess the defect density and crystallinity of carbon-based materials. The relatively high ID/IG ratio of 1.7 indicates a considerable degree of structural disorder and defects in the carbon framework, which can be attributed to the successful incorporation of S and N heteroatoms [34,35]. This elevated defect density is beneficial for the fluorescent properties of SN-CDs, as these defect sites can serve as effective emission centers [36]. The high ID/IG ratio also provides evidence for the efficient doping of S and N atoms into the carbon matrix, confirming the successful synthesis of SN-CDs.

Fig. 2b displays the infrared spectra of both SN-CDs/PASP and PASP. A clear distinction between the synthesized product and the original PASP can be observed. Similar to the single PASP, the spectra feature peaks at 1397 cm−1, 1594 cm−1, and 3432 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CN, CO, and NH bonds, respectively [37,38]. Furthermore, for SN-CDs/PASP, the distinctive peak at approximately 1122 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of the CS bond and the asymmetric vibration of the sulfonate (SO₃) group [39,40]. The band around 997 cm−1 is attributed to the symmetric stretching of the sulfonate (SO₃) moiety [41]. These band positions are typical of sulfonic acid functionalities [42,43]. Moreover, the 3432 cm−1 peak for SN-CDs/PASP is broader compared to the 3441 cm−1 peak observed in PASP, indicating that the incorporation of carbon dots introduces more amino groups and hydroxyl groups. These observations confirm that SN-CDs have successfully participated in the open-ring reaction of PSI to form SN-CDs/PASP [44,45].

To further confirm the structural details of SN-CDs and SN-CDs/PASP, XPS analysis was conducted. Fig. 3 illustrates the XPS results of SN-CDs. The data clearly show that nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) have been successfully doped into the carbon dots (CDs). Four main peaks were detected at binding energies of 285, 532, 401, and 168 eV, corresponding to C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p, respectively. In particular, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum reveals three peaks at 284.8, 286.1, and 288.9 eV, attributed to C=C/C-C, C-O/N/S, and CO, respectively. The O1s spectrum is split into two peaks at 531.8 eV and 533.1 eV, corresponding to O=C/S and OC, respectively. The N1s spectrum shows three peaks at 399.9 eV, 400.9 eV, and 401.5 eV, assigned to pyridine-type N (399.9 eV), pyrrole-type N (400.9 eV), and sp3 hybridized N (NH, 401.5 eV). This confirms successful nitrogen doping into the CDs. The S2p XPS spectrum shows two distinct peaks at 167.8 and 168.9 eV, indicating that sulfur is incorporated into the CDs in the form of C-SOx [46,47]. These results confirm the successful synthesis of SN-CDs [48,44].

Fig. 4 shows the XPS spectra of SN-CDs/PASP. Carbon (C) elements were detected at 284.8 eV (C=C/C-C), 285.9 eV (C-O/N/S), and 288.2 eV (C=O). The O 1 s spectrum primarily consists of peaks at 531.3 eV (O=C/S), 533.1 eV (CO), and 535.8 eV (an Auger peak due to the presence of sodium salt). The N 1 s spectrum is divided into three peaks at 399.0 eV, 399.9 eV, and 401.5 eV, corresponding to C-N-C, C-N-H, and N-H/N-(C)₃, respectively. The S 2p XPS spectrum reveals distinct chemical environments of sulfur in the modified material through characteristic peak splitting. The spectrum can be deconvoluted into four well-defined peaks, each providing crucial information about the sulfur bonding states. The lower binding energy doublet peaks at 163.2 eV and 164.5 eV correspond to CS 2p₃/₂ and 2p₁/₂ respectively, indicating the formation of CS covalent bonds through the modification process. In the higher binding energy region, two additional peaks at 169.6 eV and 168.3 eV are observed, which can be definitively assigned to C-SO₃ and C-SO₂ groups respectively [47]. These higher binding energy peaks demonstrate the preservation of sulfonic acid functionalities from the original carbon quantum dots. Combined with the previous infrared data, these findings confirm that PASP has been successfully modified with SN-CDs. The Na 1 s signal originates from the NaOH used in the synthesis of SN-CDs/PASP.

The morphology and size distribution of SN-CDs and SN-CDs/PASP were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 5a, pristine SN-CDs exhibit a relatively uniform spherical morphology with clear boundaries, and the particles are well-dispersed without obvious aggregation. The corresponding size distribution histogram (Fig. 5b) reveals that the diameters of SN-CDs mainly range from 8 to 15 nm, with an average diameter of 11.2 nm.

After modification with PASP (Fig. 5c), the SN-CDs maintain their spherical structure but show improved dispersion, as evidenced by the increased inter-particle distances. According to the size distribution analysis (Fig. 5d), the diameters of SN-CDs/PASP particles are concentrated between 8 and 14 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 11.8 nm, with clear lattice fringes visible, spaced approximately 0.2 nm apart [49].

The zeta potential analysis (Fig. 6a) reveals a significant enhancement in electronegativity after modifying PASP with SN-CDs, attributed to the synergistic effect of amino and sulfonic acid groups on the carbon quantum dots. The amino groups (-NH₂) and sulfonic acid groups (-SO₃H) both enhance electronegativity in alkaline environments. In basic conditions, amino groups remain in their deprotonated form, contributing to the surface charge regulation and electrostatic repulsion [50,51]. Meanwhile, sulfonic acid groups readily deprotonate to form -SO₃− ions in alkaline conditions. The negative inductive effect of the sulfonic acid group (SO bonds) promotes rapid charge transfer, enhancing the overall electronegativity of the material [52]. This stable negative charge remains consistent over a wide pH range, particularly in alkaline environments.

The increasingly negative zeta potential of SN-CDs/PASP composite compared to pristine PASP at higher pH values creates a stronger electrostatic repulsion between charged crystal particles, thereby maintaining their dispersed state and preventing aggregation. This enhanced negative surface charge not only reduces the collision probability between crystal particles but also decreases the crystal nucleation sites in solution, effectively inhibiting calcium phosphate scale formation. Moreover, the pH-dependent behavior indicates that the composite material would be especially effective in alkaline conditions where CaP scaling problems are typically more severe.

Fig. 6b shows that SN-CDs/PASP exhibit a higher specific surface area, where SN-CDs play a crucial role in enhancing the performance of composite [53]. After modification with PASP, the specific surface area of SN-CDs/PASP is further enhanced, which facilitates the adsorption of scale-forming ions and provides more active sites for interaction with the crystal surfaces [54]. The high surface area of SN-CDs/PASP allows for better dispersion and increased interaction with the surface of CaP crystals, improving the material's ability to prevent scale formation.

Fig. 7 illustrates the optical properties of the materials [55]. Fig. 7a and b shows the PL spectra of SN-CDs and SN-CDs/PASP at different wavelengths. The maximum emission peak for SN-CDs occurs at 360 nm, while for SN-CDs/PASP, it shifts to 320 nm. This difference in emission spectra is primarily attributed to the surface modification of the carbon dots by the incorporation of PASP.

Next, the relationship between the concentration of SN-CDs/PASP and emission intensity was studied at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm (Fig. 7c). As the concentration increases, the emission intensity of SN-CDs/PASP also enhances. A linear relationship was found between the emission intensity and concentration, as shown in Fig. 7d, which is crucial for real-time monitoring of SN-CDs/PASP levels in water [56].

The aqueous solution of SN-CDs/PASP emits visible blue light under a 365 nm UV lamp. Comparing the UV–vis spectra of SN-CDs and SN-CDs/PASP (Fig. 7e and f), SN-CDs show distinct absorption peaks at 208 nm and 248 nm. The peak at 208 nm corresponds to the π → π* transition of the conjugated carbon‑carbon double bonds, while the peak at 248 nm is associated with n → π* transitions, which involve surface functional groups such as CO bonds, CN bonds from nitrogen doping, and SO bonds from sulfonate groups [57–59]. These peaks indicate successful incorporation of heteroatoms into the carbon dot structure.

In the UV–vis spectrum of SN-CDs/PASP, a broad peak appears between 215 and 287 nm, signifying multiple overlapping transition processes [60]. This spectral broadening is attributed to: (1) The original π → π* transitions (215–240 nm) from the carbon‑carbon double bonds in the structures of carbon dots, modified by interactions with the PASP backbone; (2) n → π* transitions (240–270 nm) involving nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms, particularly CN bonds modified by hydrogen bonding with PASP and SO interactions with PASP's amide groups; and (3) charge transfer transitions (270–287 nm) due to electronic coupling between SN-CDs and PASP [61]. The merging of these transitions into a continuous absorption band and the shift in peak position indicate that the carbon dots have successfully functionalized and modified PASP, providing evidence for the formation of the composite material [62].

Photostability is another crucial property for high-performance fluorescent materials, and excellent photostability eliminates the need for commercial stabilizers [63]. Fig. 7g and h shows that the emission spectra of both SN-CDs and SN-CDs/PASP were monitored under 365 nm UV irradiation over different time periods (0−3 h). Both samples demonstrate outstanding photostability, maintaining consistent emission intensities and peak positions throughout the 3-h UV exposure. This exceptional stability indicates great potential for these materials in sustained optical applications.

The inhibition efficiency of SN-CDs/PASP with different raw material ratios was studied. Fig. 8a shows the scale inhibition effect on CaP (CaP) for different ratios of PSI to carbon dots, compared with the individual components. It is clear that the inhibition efficiency of the modified product is significantly higher than that of either PASP or SN-CDs alone. The best inhibition efficiency is achieved when the PSI: SN-CDs ratio is 10:1, with the scale inhibition rate for CaP approaching 98 % at a concentration of 10 mg/L, greatly improving the inhibition effect of PASP on CaP. As the ratio increases, i.e., when the PSI content rises and SN-CDs content decreases at the same concentration, the inhibition efficiency of SN-CDs/PASP decreases significantly. Therefore, subsequent experiments were conducted using SN-CDs/PASP [PSI: SN-CDs = (10:1)].
As shown in Fig. 8b, SN-CDs/PASP also demonstrates good effectiveness against both CaCO₃ and CaSO₄ scale formation, highlighting its broad applicability and multifunctional nature.

Electrochemical Nyquist and Bode plots for aluminum alloy, simulating industrial pipeline materials, in solutions containing and lacking inhibitors are shown in Fig. 8c and d. As illustrated in Fig. 8c, the Nyquist plots of the three distinct solutions demonstrate the presence of two semicircles, implying that charge transfer occurs during the electrochemical process. It is generally recognized that the high-frequency capacitance loop reflects the properties of the electrode surface deposit, offering a clear way to illustrate the influence of CaP scaling on the electrode. In comparison to the blank solution, the arc diameter is much smaller in the presence of inhibitors, which is attributed to the decreased accumulation of scale on the electrode. SN-CDs/PASP shows the smallest arc diameter, which indirectly suggests the optimal scale inhibition effect.

In contrast, the Bode plots exhibit two distinct time constants throughout the frequency spectrum. The high-frequency region is associated with the relaxation of the double-layer capacitance, while the low-frequency region reflects the relaxation process of inhibitor adsorption. Notably, the phase peak in the low-frequency region for SN-CDs/PASP shifts to lower frequencies. This shift is typically associated with an increase in charge transfer impedance, which is likely due to the adsorption of SN-CDs/PASP onto the electrode surface, where it forms a compact protective film. The narrowing of the phase angle in the high-frequency range suggests that the inclusion of SN-CDs/PASP enhances the electrode surface condition, primarily by decreasing the deposition of CaP scale.

To better understand the scale inhibition process, an appropriate equivalent circuit was used. As illustrated in Fig. 8c, the circuit model consists of three resistors (R) and two constant phase elements (CPE). Rs is representative of the resistance of the solution, Rf is the resistance of the surface film, and Rct is the charge transfer resistance. CPEf and CPEdl are used to model the surface film and double-layer capacitance at the electrode interface, respectively. It is noteworthy that Rf provides a more accurate representation of the resistance encountered during charge transfer between the electrode and the solution, directly reflecting the degree of scale accumulation on the electrode surface. The corresponding fitting values for the circuit elements are summarised in Table 1 . In the presence of SN-CDs/PASP, both Rf and Rct show the lowest resistance, indicating that CaP deposition on the electrode surface is effectively reduced.

Combining all of the above, SN-CDs/PASP acts as an effective scale inhibitor by preventing the formation of CaP scale in the solution. Additionally, it forms a compact protective film on the aluminum alloy surface, which reduces interactions between the electrode and ions in the solution [7]. As the coverage of CaP scale on the electrode surface decreases, dissolved oxygen diffuses more easily, making charge transfer faster [64]. These combined effects lead to a decrease in both Rct and Rf, improving electrochemical performance.

XRD and SEM are used to characterize the CaP (CaP) scale, specifically hydroxyapatite, and the specific surface area of PASP and SN-CDs/PASP is assessed to elucidate the scale inhibition mechanism. Fig. 9a displays the XRD spectra of CaP scales under three distinct scenarios. Taking the calcium scale from the blank solution as a reference, peaks near 2θ = 25.79° and in the range of 30°–33° correspond to the (002), (211), (112), and (300) planes of hydroxyapatite. Upon adding SN-CDs/PASP, all diffraction peaks show a noticeable reduction in intensity, suggesting a potential change in surface crystallography and possible lattice distortion.

SEM is used to examine the morphology of calcium phosphate (CaP) scales formed under various conditions. In the blank solution, the CaP scale formed (Fig. 9b, b′) exhibited a smooth, flat surface with well-defined edges, predominantly consisting of large, blocky crystals. After introducing PASP, the scale surface exhibits a slight increase in roughness, accompanied by a higher number of smaller, block-like CaP formations increased, as shown in Fig. 9c, c′. This finding indicates that PASP exerts a modest inhibitory effect on the primary growth planes of the CaP scale, albeit the impact is not particularly pronounced. In contrast, CaP scales formed in the presence of SN-CDs/PASP (Fig. 9d and d′) displayed a significant reduction in crystal size, and the overall structure became more loosely packed and porous. When considering the specific surface area data presented in Fig. 6b, it is clear that the SN-CDs/PASP composite possesses a significantly larger specific surface area than PASP alone. This increased specific surface area implies a greater reactive surface for interaction with calcium ions, resulting in more extensive adsorption and enhanced disruption of the crystal lattice, as referenced in [53,54].

Fig. 10 illustrates the probable mechanism through which SN-CDs/PASP suppresses the growth of calcium phosphate (CaP) crystals. Beyond its capacity to chelate with calcium ions to form soluble complexes, the addition of SN-CDs/PASP has two main effects. Firstly, it adsorbs onto the CaP crystals, and the electronegativity resulting from sulfur‑nitrogen doping generates an electrostatic repulsion among particles, thereby preventing their aggregation and subsequent deposition into larger particles.

Secondly, the SN-CDs/PASP interacts with the crystal surface, perturbing the structural integrity of the crystals and hindering the typical growth patterns of CaP. Moreover, the enhanced specific surface area coupled with the swift movement of the carbon dots accelerates the reaction kinetics, further amplifying the breakdown of CaP crystals. As a result, the SN-CDs/PASP proves to be exceptionally potent in preventing the formation of CaP scale.

This research involved the synthesis of sulfur‑nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (SN-CDs), which were then used to modify polyaspartic acid (PASP), leading to the successful preparation of the SN-CDs/PASP composite scale inhibitor. This composite exhibited a markedly improved capacity to inhibit calcium phosphate (CaP) scaling compared to unmodified PASP, with an inhibition efficiency nearing 98 % at a concentration of 10 mg/L. Additionally, the material's fluorescence intensity was found to have a robust linear correlation with its concentration, which suggests its potential for real-time monitoring in practical settings. Further characterization validated that the SN-CDs/PASP composite effectively disrupted the crystalline structure of CaP. Overall, these observations support that the SN-CDs/PASP composite is a promising candidate for scale inhibition and provides significant insights for the advancement of next-generation scale inhibitors.

Yijie Cai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Daquan Zhang: Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Dongdong Liu: Validation. Jiawei Hou: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the NSFC project (Grant No. 52071198) and the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (Grant No. 18DZ2204400, 19DZ2271100).
